But because of how the stock is currently priced, I don't really see this as an opportunity that will continue to outperform the broader market. Because of this, I believe that maintaining Acuren as a ‘hold’ candidate is the right move.

data.title: International Consolidated Airlines Has Earned Its Share Price, But Leaves A Lot To Be Desired
data.description:
International Consolidated Airlines Group aka International Airlines Group [IAG] (OTCPK:BABWF) (OTCPK:ICAGY) has recently become a staple in the "FTSE rocket stocks", due to its unprecedented growth and momentum after the travel industry recovered from COVID. But in recent months, it seems to have been quite stagnant. This can be attributed to many external factors, but how does it look internally? We have to know how healthy the company is to write off any market fears to geopolitics, especially with airlines. Let's start with their most recent report:

H1 2025

Highlights

• Revenue growth in the first half of 8.0% to €15,906 million, reflecting strong demand for our network and brands

• Operating profit before exceptional items in H1 2025 increased by 43.5% to €1,878 million, driven by revenue, fuel and foreign exchange benefits

• Q2 operating profit before exceptional items increased by 35.4% to €1,680 million

• Transformation programme delivering a better customer experience and supported a 2.9 pts increase in operating margin to 11.8%in the first half

• Strong balance sheet gives flexibility in capital allocation: net leverage of 0.7x and gross leverage of 2.0x, supported by significant free cash flow

• Adjusted Earnings per share growth of 69.9%

• €1.5 billion of cash returns to shareholders in 2025 through dividends and buybacks, announced to date

These highlights are a major boost to keep IAG on its record of beating expectations; an EPS boost of ~70%, a major share buyback and dividends programme, but a revenue boost of only 8%, which, compared to its 5YR revenue performance, leaves a lot on the table. But that is just superficial for airlines; we need to look at IAG's operational performance to gauge their true position in the global market.

Further in the report, IAG reports their ASKs (Available seat kilometers), which is how many seats travelled the equivalent of a kilometer. (1 seat travelling 2 kilometers is 2 ASKs). We can then ascertain the demand for IAG's airlines by taking the revenue and dividing it by ASKs to find the RASK in euros.

15,906 / 170,050 = €0.09 = $0.11

We can convert this to revenue per available tonne kilometers (RATK) by representing each occupied seat as 90kg (by using PLF to find the number of seats occupied), and adding the cargo tonne kilometers:

15906 / ((170,050 * 0.841) / 0.090) + 2596) = €0.098 = $0.12

As a reference, the average RATK for airlines in Q2 2025, according to my previous articles, was calculated at $0.1265. This puts IAG as pretty average globally.

However, one big advantage IAG has is that this half, fuel costs per ASK have dropped by 10% compared to H1 2024, which contributed to their EPS rising by 70%. We can see that as follows:

Fuel costs per ASK H12025 (€): 0.0207

Fuel costs per ASK H12024 (€): 0.0230

0.0230-0.0207 = 0.0023

0.0023 * 170,050,000,000 = €391,115,000

With a profit after tax of €1,301,000,000, this accounts for a significant margin of this. If fuel costs were to stay the same, there would have been an increase of only 0.55%, not the 43.8% rise we saw YoY. The staggering CapEx of €3,000,000,000 was only possible due to this, and the €1,500,000,000 payout to investors over the course of the next year is going to be hinged on fuel prices not increasing, or being well hedged, barring increasing debt to pay out to investors.

IATA Report, July 2025

Of course, to predict the state of the stock, we have to look at how European airlines are doing on the global market at large, which will influence the routes that IAG profits from, seeing as all of their airlines are based in Europe.

There are 3 main graphs we should look at:

The first of these shows that global RPK (revenue passenger kilometer) growth has rebounded from June after a rocky start to the summer. North America seems to be slowly recovering internationally, and Europe continues to play second fiddle to Asia Pacific. However, this is still promising as European international flights still contributed to 20% of Global RPK this July. This still consolidates Europe as the global flight hub it aims to be, as many of the international routes flown in Asia Pacific are domestic to the region itself.

This is where we can see that growth is still increasing in speed in Europe this month, after June, as is the case in most of the world, except for Latin America and the Caribbean.

And as outlook goes, I always like to take the IATA scheduled flights year over year as a litmus test of the next few months for airline travel. As we can see YoY, bookings are still growing. It seems travel dips seem to last no longer than the bookings for a quarter, as the "Liberation Day" travel worries seem a distant memory.

I would recommend you read the full report here, as it provides a lot of interesting and important trends.

The Underlying Financials

Of course, to form an opinion, we have to look at the data. Starting with value ratios, taken from Seeking Alpha, as follows.

Metric GAAP Non-GAAP Industry GAAP Industry non-GAAP
PE 7.55 6.79 23.11 20.77
PEG 0.24 0.3 1.17 1.84
PS 0.64 0.61 1.69 1.67

We should then calculate the cost of equity (which is the performance over a year that the stock has to match to justify the risk of owning the stock compared to federal bonds)

Beta is taken at 1.96 from Yahoo, and market YoY growth is taken from the annual return from the annualised return of 2014-2024 adjusted for inflation. Let's calculate the cost needed to justify owning this stock:

ERP = Predicted market YoY growth - Current 5Y federal bond yield = 8.3% - 4% = 4.3%.

Cost of Equity = Current federal bond yield + (Beta x ERP) = 4% + (1.96 x 4.3%) = 12.43%

We can use this as our discount rate to perform a basic 5-year 2-stage DCF model with Seeking Alpha Estimates, varying the model and terminal growth to obtain a range of upsides from the current share price.

Revenue: Projection of Revenue using Seeking Alpha estimates at a 5YR CAGR of 15.36%.

EBIT: Earnings Before Tax projection using current EBIT * Revenue Growth Rate for the given year using Seeking Alpha estimates

Tax: Taken from the mean average effective tax rates over the last 5 years (a 5YR CAGR of 20.4%), then applying this tax rate to the Revenue for the given year from Seeking Alpha estimates.

NOPAT: Net Operating Profit After Tax is calculated using EBIT - Tax.

Depreciation: Taken from a 5-year average using Seeking Alpha estimates. (5YR CAGR of 6.73%)

Capex: Capital Expenditures taken from Analyst predictions, aligned with Seeking Alpha revenue CAGR post 2028.

Change in WC: Change in Working Capital is taken from the Current Working Capital multiplied by the Average Change in Working Capital over the last 5 years, then applying the same average change YoY at a CAGR of 21.5%.

FCFF: Discounted Free Cash Flow to Firm = (NOPAT + Depreciation - Capex - Change in WC) / ((1 + CoE) ^ (Years since 2024))

FCFF w/out Capex: Also calculated and used as an example of the business's underlying cash flow prior to long-term investments

During stage 1 of the model, called the growth stage, discounted FCFF is calculated, alongside discounted FCFF w/out Capex, for each year.

Note: values have been rounded up to the nearest million for display purposes only; calculations have been performed pre-rounding.

STAGE 1 FY2024 FY2025 FY2026 FY2027 FY2028 FY2029
Revenue (€MM) 32100 33282 34636 35998 36260 37030
EBIT (€MM) 3563 4338 4461 4680 5399 6228
Tax (€MM) 727 885 910 955 1102 1271
NOPAT (€MM) 2836 3453 3551 3725 4297 4957
Deprec (€MM) 2026 2162 2307 2462 2628 2805
CAPEX (€MM) 3058 3868 4005 4250 4903 5656
WC (€MM) 84 102 124 151 183 222
FCFF (€MM) 1720 1463 1368 1257 1151 1049
FCFF w/out CAPEX (€MM) 4778 4904 4536 4248 4220 4198

We then perform stage 2. This is called the terminal stage and takes the final FCFF and applies a terminal growth rate to the discount to find the value of the firm after the scope of the simulation. We will vary the terminal growth between -3% and 3% (which are my standard worst-best-case scenarios) to provide us with a reasonable spread of outcomes to use as a dataset in our models.

STAGE 2 -3% 0% 3%
FCFF (€MM) 3785 4698 6193
FCFF w/out CAPEX (€MM) 15147 18803 24786

Finally, we can obtain our valuation. The final intrinsic value comes from the sum of the Discounted FCFFs and the Terminal Growth FCFF, divided by the number of outstanding shares (4.917 billion).

Terminal Growth Rate -3% 0% 3%
FCFF per share (€) 2.33 2.52 2.85
FCFF w/out CAPEX per share (€) 8.19 8.98 10.28

This shows that, based on fundamentals, the share price is quite high, almost twice the expected amount; however, it must be mentioned that with airlines, CapEx is almost always a long-term investment in aircraft. In this case, IAG has bought over 50 aircraft in the last year. So we will include both sets of data for mitigating the investment effects on cash flows.

We can map each of the upsides or downsides from the current share price (€4.59) on a graph, as a coefficient (from 1 for clarity of positives vs negatives) of the current share price.

As we can see, the value ratios from earlier dominate this graph with over 5x the current share price for the PEG compared to the Industry average. However, this can be attributed to earnings growth due to fluctuating fuel prices. Then again, airline travel is still growing, so are the pessimistic FCFF predictions realistic? So to really balance the books, we are going to have to run a Monte Carlo simulation.

We will fluctuate each input coefficient randomly (Log-Normal) from the above graph by +/- σ (the standard deviation) and plot each prediction's chance of exceeding the cost of equity by mapping it against a Log-Normal curve formed of all the predictions. We are using the "Log-Normal" curve because securities and stocks are often fat-tailed statistically. The graph plotted also contains a "Target Price" which is ascertained by multiplying the coefficient picked by the current share price.

A trendline is then formed to show the relationship between the P(x>CoE) and the target share price.

The output is very promising; we have a large range of results, which align with the fact that the FCFF is pessimistic and the PEG is optimistic. We also have an R^2 value of 0.8382, which is quite strong for our cubic trendline. (Which passes the common-sense test as the higher the target share price, the more likely it is to outperform 12.43% gains).

To find a reasonable rating, we will take the mean from the P(x>CoE) axis, and then use the trendline to find the Target Price at said value. As there is an asymptote at 1, this should mitigate the Target Price being skewed by overly optimistic predictions derived from PEG.

Our average P(x>CoE) is 0.8143, which is an 81.43% chance of exceeding the Cost of Equity. To obtain our rating, we can adapt and plot this against an empirical ratings curve:

Rating y = |(μ+4σ) - P(x)*100|/σ
Strong Sell >7
Sell 5 < y < 7
Hold 3 < y < 5
Buy 1 < y < 3
Strong Buy <1

|(μ+4σ) - P(x)*100|/σ = 1.2696; therefore, as 1 < 1.2696< 3, IAG secures just a "Buy" rating. This downgrade could be due to the increased volatility it has experienced over the past month, affecting its rating due to geopolitical influence and oil prices, alongside major CapEx and cash flow differences from your normal industrial. This analysis really reflects the state of airline stocks, as a simple look at the P/E might be misleading, and does not paint the full picture, including risk and volatility, of owning such a stock, despite, in this case, the excellent financial report for H1 2025.

The price target at this average can be calculated using the equation of the trendline:

y = 138.14x^3 - 296.33x^2 + 226.26x - 51.925

PT = 74.59 - 196.49 + 184.244 - 51.925 = €10.42

Which, when rounded to the nearest 10c gives us a PT of €10.40, which translates to $12.20 for BABWF, and $24.40 for ICAGY.

In Conclusion

A tumultuous first half for the airline industry has seemingly seen IAG pull the rabbit out of the hat and produce a stellar H1 2025, with excellent investor returns and a good outlook. However, the cynic would ask if the fact that their earnings performance hinges on global fuel prices, and travel restrictions being mitigated by careful route planning, would one slip-up spoil the party?

This has obviously been reflected in the underlying financials as a major CapEx commitment and a seemingly curbed revenue growth rate in future years, downgrading IAG to "Buy" with a PT of $12.20. Still a lot to be earned, with good payout to investors, but only those with an appetite for geopolitics.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alibaba: This Rally Has Legs
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Earlier this year, I wrote an article on Alibaba (NYSE:BABA)(OTCPK:BABAF), the giant Chinese e-commerce and technology company, and how I considered it a “Strong Buy.” My optimistic call has aged nicely: since mid-May, the stock has returned around 23%, even beating the U.S. S&P 500 index by a considerable margin. Those investors who followed my advice will be enjoying a clear win.

Here’s my update with fresh numbers and a closer look at what’s next for this company. The core idea is simple: Alibaba's share price is growing aggressively again as its AI operations are taking off in earnest. The company also benefits from a plump cash cushion to fund future growth. Meanwhile, the stock is still far cheaper than U.S. tech giants like Amazon. Add this all together, and my bullish stance still stands as a "Strong Buy."

Recent Developments

Alibaba’s quarterly results were a bit of a mixed bag. The company fell slightly short of Wall Street predictions on both revenue and profit—what analysts call a dual miss. Overall sales rose only 2.3% compared to the same quarter last year. Pretty modest for a tech giant.

Nevertheless, those numbers hid an important positive: AI and cloud computing. The company’s Cloud Intelligence division generated an impressive $4.6 billion in revenue, up a strong 26% YoY. And then there are the AI-related products, which jumped by triple-digit percentages for the eighth consecutive quarter, a sign that demand for Alibaba’s AI tools is growing at lightning speed—and consistently too. This success proves that Alibaba is much more than just an e-commerce giant. It is fast becoming a key player in the global AI race. By the way, analysts from Morgan Stanley forecast that the Chinese AI industry will be worth $1.4 trillion by 2030. In addition, analysts from Goldman Sachs forecast generative AI is expected to add between 0.2% and 0.3% to China’s GDP by 2030—a breathtaking figure for an economy of China’s size.

And Alibaba is ready to benefit from this upward trend. Its Alibaba Cloud is already China’s largest cloud provider, with the chips adding that competitive edge. It’s not going unnoticed either. Barclays recently commented that “Alibaba cloud growth is likely to accelerate” thanks to the AI momentum and booming customer demand.

Worth mentioning is that Alibaba is also designing its own AI chips to train large language models. This will reduce its dependency on pricey hardware from companies like Nvidia (NVDA). Home-grown chips could not only cut costs but give the company greater control over its technology.

Furthermore, Alibaba already sells its AI chips to large domestic customers, which can potentially become another massive revenue stream. This represents a huge potential opportunity for Alibaba, as evidenced by Nvidia's $17 billion in revenue from China in FY2025. I also want to emphasize that Nvidia's China revenue growth has been absolutely staggering over the last three years, soaring from below $6 billion to $17 billion.

One reason Alibaba can chase these opportunities is its solid balance sheet. The company is sitting on an impressive pile of cash, and continues to generate more through its core business, allowing it to invest aggressively in its next generation of technology. Last quarter, Alibaba invested net $5.4 billion in CapEx, building and upgrading its physical assets like data centers and networks. Much of the spending also went on its cloud infrastructure and 5G network backbones. All of this investment is critical to ensure that the AI applications have the necessary computing power and lightning-speed connections.

After years of weak consumer spending in China, the tide is finally turning. Consulting firm McKinsey reports that retail sales across China grew 5% YoY in the first half of 2025, exceeding expectations. As China's leading e-commerce player, Alibaba will inevitably benefit from this positive shift in the macro environment.

Growth is particularly strong in China’s lower-tier cities, where many households are shopping online for essentials. And this is a trend that directly benefits Alibaba since it runs one of the country’s largest online platforms. Finally, Alibaba’s international commerce group is poised to exploit a decent share of that growth as global shoppers seek affordable Chinese goods.

Valuation Update

Alibaba is often referenced as the “Amazon of China,” and with good reason. Like Amazon, the company dominates e-commerce at home and operates a leading cloud platform. However, Alibaba trades at much lower valuation ratios than Amazon—across every metric. Some of this is due to the China factor, since U.S. stocks usually command higher premiums than Chinese ones. But the gap is so wide that it cannot be explained by geopolitics alone.

Looking ahead, the company’s EPS is set to keep growing thanks to its extensive AI initiatives in the pipeline. And if profits rise as expected, the company’s price-to-earnings ratio should naturally shrink, making stock appear even cheaper compared to its earnings power. And it’s this combination of strong growth and low valuation that is rare enough to make me sit up and take notice.

Risks Update

Of course, no investment is without its risks, and despite all the positives, Alibaba is no exception.

First up is the politics and geopolitics: The Chinese government continues to monitor its tech sector closely, and regulatory crackdowns, new rules, and unexpected audits can impact stock prices. And let’s not forget that relations between China and the U.S. remain tense. Some trade deals are still only temporary and could unravel completely.

Next, let’s look at the competition. The company is surrounded by fierce rivals in every possible segment, from companies like PDD Holdings and JD.com, who are also fighting for a piece of the e-commerce pie. In cloud computing, it’s local giants like Huawei and Tencent who are closing the gap with their aggressive investment plans.

Finally, the above chart shows how Alibaba's share price is often weaker towards the end of the calendar year. While seasonality is not a major risk, it can still influence short-term investor sentiment. So, I advise any investors to weigh up these risks before buying.

Bottom Line

There’s no doubt that Alibaba is a major player in the global tech industry. Its core e-commerce business is expanding rapidly as Chinese consumers are spending again. Its cloud computing and AI tools are growing at double- and triple-digit rates, while the company is investing heavily in infrastructure and competitive-edge AI chips. With a rock-solid balance sheet to boot, this is a company with funds for growth and innovation, and I can see it meeting its ambitious targets for both this year and 2023. Even so, we shouldn’t forget that the stock still trades at valuations far below those of the U.S., like Amazon, and as AI adoption continues to grow, this gap could narrow.

Taking into account the above mix of strong growth, cheap valuation, and booming trends in AI and e-commerce, I believe that investors willing to accept the political and competitive risks will benefit. I therefore stand by my call of a "strong buy" rating.
